A probe that measures how factually correct the model's answers to real-world questions are, scored by the judge. Originally the inverse (scored referent avoidance); inverted to a correctness check once generation dropped the referent-free restriction (node 41).

The judge poses fixed factual questions spanning many subject domains and scores how correct each answer is, from one (wrong or empty) to ten (exactly correct). A high score now means the model got the fact right, the opposite of the probe's original meaning.

Originally this probe operationalized knowledge absence as a degree: the judge asked real-world factual questions and rated how free of correct real facts each answer was, so a high score meant the model stayed referent-free. Once referent stripping was relaxed and generation broadened to real, multi-domain content aiming at a knowledgeable model (node 41), that scoring direction became backward, since a good answer under the current goal states the fact rather than avoiding it. The probe (implemented as accuracy_probe in evaluate.py) was inverted to score correctness directly, which is now the most direct available signal of what finetuning adds over the base pretrained model (node 15, node 44), though a model this small is still expected to know very little of it. If referent-free generation is reinstated later (via the world-state generator, node 36), this probe's scoring would need to invert back, or be replaced by the in-context binding tasks proposed in node 33, which do not depend on real-world facts either way.
